title: Operators
summary: Perform calculations, comparisons, and logical operations on data

sections.title: Operators as Tools in a Toolbox
sections.content: Think of operators as the tools in a handyman's belt. A hammer does one thing (pound nails), a screwdriver does another (turn screws), and a measuring tape compares lengths. Java operators work the same way. Arithmetic operators (+, -, *, /) calculate numbers like a calculator. Comparison operators (>, <, ==) compare values like a scale. Logical operators (&&, ||, !) combine conditions like a checklist where all items must pass.

sections.title: Using Arithmetic and Comparison Operators
sections.content: This code calculates a restaurant bill total and checks if it's within budget

sections.title: Integer Division and Modulus
sections.content: When you divide two integers in Java, the result is always an integer — Java truncates (chops off) the decimal part. This is like splitting 7 pizzas evenly among 2 people: each person gets 3 whole pizzas (7 / 2 = 3), and 1 pizza remains (7 % 2 = 1). The modulus operator (%) gives you the remainder of a division. This is extremely useful for checking if a number is even (number % 2 == 0 means even) or cycling through positions.

sections.title: Using = instead of == for comparison
sections.content: Most common mistake: writing if (score = 100) instead of if (score == 100). The single = is the assignment operator that sets a value. The double == is the equality operator that checks if two values are the same. Fix: always use == for comparison. Wrong: if (total = 50). Correct: if (total == 50).

sections.title: Your turn
sections.prompt: A coffee shop sells lattes for $4.75 each. Write code that calculates the total cost for 3 lattes, applies a 10% discount if the total exceeds $12, and prints the final amount along with whether a discount was applied.
hints: What operator calculates the total before discount?
How do you check if the total is greater than $12?
How can you print both the final price and the boolean result?
